segments.text: Oh, we're back.
Yes, sir.
Jumping up and down.
Sorry.
Good morning, thank you
all.
All right, thank you.
Thank you. Thank you.
Very good. All right. Good morning, Minister.
Welcome along to the estimates hearing for Vote Defense and Vote Defense Force.
Your first time in the main chair for this, appreciate
that, and congratulations again on that opportunity.
Welcome also to Chief of Defense Force, Air Marshal Tony Davies, and
Secretary of Defense, Brooke Berrington, and your respective teams.
We have two hours set down for the hearing today.
We welcome comments from you. You'll have seen the
proposed agenda, giving a broad outline of the sections we'd
like to cover. We'll work our way through that, but of course,
maintaining flexibility to focus on areas of priority for
members of the committee as we go. But I'd like to hand over to you for some
introductory comments.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. Morena, good morning to all the members of the committee.
I'm really delighted to have this opportunity to tell the stories of
the wonderful work that Defense does for New
Zealand. Of course, I'm delighted to be here with the Chief of
Defense Force and the Secretary of Defense, as you've rightly acknowledged.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. And of course,
right behind us, so to speak, but also quite literally, I'll have them to excuse my
back,
many good men and women of the New Zealand Defense Force, the Ministry of
Defense. And I'd be remiss if I didn't start by acknowledging the work
that our uniformed personnel, along with Defense civilians, and of course, MOD
team, do in defense of our nation.
I'm grateful personally to them, of course, on behalf of government, and I know
that across the Parliament there's a strong and rightful recognition of
that. And can I say, I've always had really good interactions
with this committee. I do want to acknowledge, if you'll indulge me for a moment,
relatively new in his counterpart role, Honorable Damien O'Connor.
And for the last time that we'll do this together, Mr. Gregor O'Connor.
So thank you for the opportunity to interact with the committee on these important
matters. I'll keep my opening remarks really brief, because obviously I welcome the
opportunity for the questions and comments that the committee may have.
But through you, Mr. Chair, I'd also respectfully suggest that if we were to
give an equivalent opportunity to both of these gentlemen, that would be helpful
to set the scene. In terms of the recent budget, which is, of course,
nominally the focus of today's session, I was pleased that Budget
2026 reflected the government's commitment to
support Defense to do the important work that it does.
Of course, there was the headline figure of the $1.58
billion expenditure, broken down to $700 and
$880 million worth of capital and operating
expenditure. And of course, within that, a number of key aims need to
be met.
Some of those revolve around new platforms
for the particular effects, and we can obviously talk about that in
as much detail as you wish. But of course, also investing in our people
is crucially important. So I was pleased that we have been
able to state clearly that we will focus on
ensuring that we retain, particularly retain, yes, continue to recruit, but
also retain the
sailors, soldiers, and aviators that we need to continue to deliver the
important effects
through our Defense Force. And included in that is, of course,
continuing to improve the defense estate and infrastructure, particularly where
accommodation is concerned. That is an aspect of the
preparedness of our people to serve, so it's important that we continue to invest
strongly in that regard. I will say that for me personally, that's
something of a passion project. I was very pleased to have been given
the delegation, among a couple of others, for defense
estate and infrastructure in my previous role as Associate Minister of Defense, of
course, under our colleague and friend, former colleague now, the Honorable
Judith Collins, of course, in her new role as Minister of Defense.
So I'm pleased to be able to
do my honest attempt to continue to carry on that good
work. So with that, Mr. Chair, through you, I will
say again that I look forward to interacting with yourselves.
And I look actually across the committee, and I see no fewer than three
fellow parliamentarians who have worn the uniform in one
color or another, including a couple of ex-Army Territorial
Force officers and, of course, ex-Wing Commander Crossley.
So to you and of course, all members of the committee, look forward to our
discussion today.
Thank you.
Did you wish to have opening remarks from the Secretary of Defense?
Yeah, if it's okay with you, I'll pass to Chief of Defense Force
and or the Minister, excuse me, Secretary of Defense.
Okay, shall I go?
You go.
Good morning, Mr. Chair and members.
Thank you for this opportunity to address the committee.
Ministry of Defense has been tasked with preparing and equipping a defense force
that can respond to the various challenges that New Zealand may face.
Whether that's supporting communities impacted by severe weather events or
deploying in support of our collective security interests,
it's a responsibility we take seriously.
That's why we are focused on three things, three main things.
Delivering the 2025 Defense Capability Plan
Working with our partners to ensure that our bonds, which are based on
trust, are strong and resilient, and working with
industry to deliver capability faster, but without losing
rigor.
We are focused on delivering existing defense projects to time, while
providing value for money and innovative thinking.
And we are also progressing the first of the two-year reviews of the Defense
Capability Plan in 2027.
It's what we're focused on at the moment.
I now hand over to the Chief of the Defense Force.
Thank you, Secretary, and thank you to the Chair and to committee for the
opportunity to be here today and to make some opening remarks.
The New Zealand Defense Force is responding to a more volatile, a
more contested, and a less predictable world
by sharpening our focus on readiness,
our operational delivery, and working very closely with our partners.
Keeping our core purpose firmly in focus
is critical in order for us to provide the right
options to government, credible combat
capable options.
That means behind the scenes, lifting readiness across the force,
embedding a stronger war fighting ethos, and preparing for more
demanding operations alongside our partners if
needed.
At the same time, the New Zealand Defense Force continues to
deliver at pace. We are active across domestic,
regional, and global operations.
We're protecting our maritime approaches.
We're contributing to
longstanding, ongoing international missions.
And we are responding whenever New Zealanders need us most.
These tasks increasingly overlap,
and they require a defense force that is flexible,
responsive, and able to shift quickly between
roles and tasks.
We are rebuilding and strengthening the foundations of the Defense Force.
Our people are central to everything we do,
and effort is going into stabilizing the workforce,
generating critical skills, and ensuring our personnel and their families
are properly supported, because readiness ultimately
depends on them.
Alongside this, we are bringing in new capabilities and making the
most of recent investments.
This includes new technologies,
strengthening maritime capability in particular, and
increasing our interoperability with partners through
both training and operations.
All of this is being delivered with a clear focus on urgency,
but also
value for money,
and ensuring effort and resources are not wasted
and directed where they have the greatest impact on
operational readiness.
In short,
the NZDF is actively adapting to meet the demands
set by government,
delivering today while building the capability and readiness
required for the challenges ahead. Thank you.
Very good. All right. Thank you very much. We'll jump straight into it.
So now with the overview of appropriations, first question from the Honorable
David.
Look, thank you very much and welcome to the committee, and
congratulations on getting more money, I guess, for defense in
a very tight budget.
But as was said, one of the big challenges is retention.
That is the personnel, which is the heart and soul of course, of
any defense force.
And when I look down at the additional funding that has gone in,
$43.5 million
for cost increases just to develop a plan,
and only $30 million
for remuneration.
So my question to you is, have we still got our priorities wrong in actually
looking after the people on the ground,
their salaries, their conditions, and in fact, their housing, which is
acknowledged,
the basic conditions, is still something that we are undercooking,
or you are in the budget in our approach?
Yeah. Thank you very much for the question.
I think it's a very
reasonable and appropriate line of questioning, that we
must ensure that our personnel are sufficiently well looked after to
wish to continue to serve. Of course, that's not just a matter of
doing the right thing by our people, but of course, the personnel
whom we rely upon to operate in the
different domains
will enable us to be operationally effective.
I would say that across the four-year
envelope of funding identified in the budget, that with
$174 million to meet the cost increase to ensure that the
personnel and equipment are ready to deploy,
along with 120 million to bring Defense Force pay closer to
market rates, and I emphasize closer, point to which I'll return briefly
finally.
And also to meet non-discretionary remuneration increases, that which is
effectively automatic or determined externally.
I think it's fair to say that we have made a visible commitment in this
space. Of course, it's always possible to say that more can and
should be done. But for now, I'm pleased that we are making
steps in the right direction.
And more particularly on the question of pay rates being brought
closer to market rates, it seems to me that for
reasons of
funding levels over many years, successive governments,
that we have some catching up to do.
I'm pleased that we are making steps in the right direction.
But as with the Defense Capability Plan itself, broadly across all
the different categories of investment that we need, we identify that
ongoing work is needed. And indeed, that has been the commitment of the government
through Defense Capability Plan 2025, to
continue to increase as necessary to ensure that those defense aims are met
As I say, with people being an important part, and as you've quite rightly
acknowledged along the same lines.
Can I
then just step back from that, highlighting the importance, I guess, of people all
the way through.
Defense Capability Plan, very bold, ambitious statements made at
the launch of that, and I know now the minister's
moved on to
greener pastures and left the mantle with you.
2% of GDP is an ambitious task,
and it's a lot of money.
How well are you going in
maintaining that trajectory?
Yeah . Thanks for the question. Of course, it's important that we do as we
say, and following those important statements made by my
predecessor on behalf of government with the release of Defense Capability Plan
2025, hereafter DCP.
I think it's fair to say that we know that we need to
ensure that our actions match our words.
So far, that has been the case, with around the
1.2% of GDP mark now representing the trajectory,
very much tracking the line that you would expect between two points of,
roughly speaking, 1% of GDP, to the 2%
mark in the year 2032, 2033. So we are on the right path,
but we do need to stay that course, and we're conscious of that.
Supplementary from-
Yes
... the supplementary on that.
Just acknowledging the comments my colleague's made around the
defense estate. I think it's quite timely as well, and it's been brought up by the
committee a number of times. My supplementary is around
what plans you have in place to making sure that we can retain
soldiers, sailors, aviators,
and trying to insulate them from
getting overseas to Australia, given the Australian Defense Force has got superior
pay and better conditions of service.
Yeah. Thank you very much. I think,
again, if I may say, a really astute question.
We're very mindful of the need to recruit well
and
it might be that the committee would wish to hear from the Chief of Defense
Force and/or chief people officer in terms of personnel numbers
coming in.
I won't steal their thunder except to say only that there is a good story to be
told there. In terms of then retaining personnel, including with
the potential attractiveness of other options within the New
Zealand workforce or indeed overseas, I think there are a number of different ways
that we need to approach that question.
One is that we need to make it worth a young
Kiwi, or indeed a mid-career
worker who might wish to
see a life in service as having great benefits for them
at a later stage of life. To think about the reasons that they join, and
to make sure that it's worthwhile, but also that they will be properly
resourced and equipped to do the things that they consider worthwhile.
And part of that does go to deployments and other opportunities,
recognizing they put their hand up to serve specifically for that
reason. In terms of looking after them well,
again, between us already, we've touched on some themes around
housing. But I'd also say the balance of the
estate in terms of training and even operational
facilities need to be invested in.
There is a considerable work program along those lines.
For example, in relation to Devonport Naval Base.
And clearly, we need to be mindful of the need to offer
opportunities too, in terms of people being well
skilled and equipped for doing the role that they can within the New Zealand
Defense Force. Of course, I don't want to undermine the essential point from a
defense point of view that we need to have
smart, talented, hardworking individuals, and we do, operating in
teams, of course. But I also think that the Defense Force,
when it plays a role for the benefit of New Zealand Inc.
of providing values and ethics and
work ethic and particular skills that are valuable to us as a whole,
then when they leave the service and go to do other things,
on New Zealand soil, we hope,
that's a net benefit to New Zealand as well.
A quick supplementary, Tim Costley.
Yeah. Encouraging, Minister, listening to some of these questions.
I agree with your point it's not just money. They want an adventure, right?
We've got to give it to them. But are you open to sort of
cross-party support to get all parties coming
out and saying, "Yes, we want to see more funding for defense.
We support that significant injection, that 2%.
We
support deployments." Is that-
Yeah. I think that's a really good question.
I will say that I've been very pleased with the
nature of the interaction and also the tone of the interaction with opposition
parties. Opposition with a capital O, only in a technical sense within the
parliamentary context, of course.
I think there is a broad agreement across the political aisle at the moment,
reflecting the social license that's now present within the New Zealand
public for
properly equipping our Defense Force to do things that are important in the
increasingly uncertain and dangerous world in which we live.
I would say, of course, that the point that
underlies Mr. Costley's question is a very important one.
I think it would be helpful if we continue to see that degree of
support
for our Defense Force, recognizing, of course, not
only that there is value in a deterrent effect
being available when, of course, we ourselves, and
in conjunction with allies, partners, and friends, demonstrate that we're able
to defend our ideals and interests.
But also, of course, recognizing all the other ways in which the
Defense Force helps to keep New Zealand and
our cousins in the Pacific, and others around the world, safe.
An example, of course, would be search and rescue operations.
I think sometimes we make the mistake of considering these to be business as usual,
but of course for the-
Souls who are saved by
maritime patrol aircraft identifying, for example,
those who are stricken at sea, and save lives,
sometimes of New Zealanders, sometimes of other nationalities.
These are hugely important roles that our defence force can play.
So the ongoing support for defence to play those roles,
I think would be very welcome indeed.
I think our men and women in uniform particularly always notice,
and are appreciative when support is forthcoming, in a
non-political way, but of course, from the political level of government
decision-making.
At this point, I wonder actually, Mr.
Chair, if you'll allow me to pass to the Chief of Defence Force actually to provide
a real life worked example of very recent past
along exactly those lines.
Please.
Thank you. Just summarizing several of the minister's questions about
people,
I would assure the committee that the attrition
of several years ago, largely driven by COVID,
through the COVID period,
has stabilized.
Between our uniformed and civilian
workforce at the moment, we are sitting at just under
8.5%, and it's been like that for the last year.
And we think that that's a reasonably healthy
level of turnover for an organization
or a workforce, a defence force.
The
fact that the attrition has slowed doesn't mean it won't stay low
forever. There are periodic and cyclic draws and
demands which will see that fluctuate.
The problem we're left with, though, is that that high attrition left
some major gaps
throughout our sailors, soldiers, and aviators, right from
the junior
levels of people that are fully trained and competent to do their jobs, right up
to our most senior people. The challenge
for us now is to fill those gaps and you
can't just obviously go out and
recruit
a ship's captain
or a P-8 pilot or a head of engineering
in the machine shop,
and expect them to be able to deploy a few minutes later.
They need to be grown from the ground up.
So we're putting quite a lot of effort into
recruiting the right people as fast as we can within the parameters that are set
for us, which includes budget, but also on
retention. Quite a few of these measures that
appear in the Budget '26 are going
into
focusing on retention.
But it's a very delicate balance, and it needs careful
prioritization. Every dollar we get needs to be split
five ways between people,
between equipment,
infrastructure that's aged, failing, or we need new types of
infrastructure,
and
operating itself, as well as software and systems like that.
So it's a very
difficult
equation to balance, and there is never enough resource to go in.
We can't just put all our money into people is what I'm saying.
We still need to be able to operate.
And then finally, it's not just
pay that retains people, although it's very important.
As
Mr. Kosti said, it's the conditions of service,
it's deployments,
it's sense of purpose and job satisfaction.
There's the people factors around teamwork and morale and leadership.
So there's a lot that goes into retention other than just pay
in the pocket, although that is fundamentally important.
Right. Was your question in relation to the overview or
Yeah, it was sort of a follow-up on one of the comments made.
Okay.
So, Minister,
as we're aware of the comments made by the Americans around pushing us
up to 3.5% spend as well,
I think the term they used is freeloader.
And so I'm wondering what you've got in place to insulate us from
those types of expectations,
from allies, partners, friends,
in terms of the cost of living crisis that we're in.
As you know, where every dollar we spend someplace means there's less of a dollar
to spend on our crumbling health structure,
support needed in schools, so on and so forth.
So I wonder if you've got any plan on how to, first of all, manage that situation
with them.
But secondly, if you've got any plans that you can share with the
committee.
Yeah . Thank you very much. Clearly, there's a lot in that question.
Perhaps I can start by acknowledging the fundamental point that social
license is important, and we've touched on that already, in
terms of the understanding of the New Zealand public about the world in which we're
living and the importance of playing our part, including through defense.
Clearly, there is an opportunity cost for every dollar that's spent on defense as
compared with health or education services, as you've alluded
to. Obviously, I wouldn't agree with your characterization of our health
system, but that's another story for another day and another select committee.
For the purpose of making sure we invest
in defense, clearly the
operational outputs are
the key driver, but actually, I think it is possible to say that we are able to
achieve more than one aim at once, to the extent that we
see additional defense investment as an opportunity for New Zealand defense
industry. I think the economic growth potential, including through skills,
employment, including in our regions-
Is considerable. And if we take a
thoughtful approach to the additional expenditure,
then it will be possible to realize benefits for the New
Zealand public that go beyond the
straightforward, but obviously significant
outcomes that can be achieved under what you would traditionally think of as a
defense heading. Certainly through the defense industry strategy, we
outlined our intention to do exactly that, and there's detail
that we can get into, if you should wish,
about encouraging New Zealand industry in itself,
both to provide sovereign
and homegrown
ways of providing to our defense force.
Of course, if we can encourage them and assist them to export to
like-minded nations, I hasten to add, to provide
their
services and suppliers elsewhere, that's a good thing as well.
And of course, again, those general economic benefits that accrue when we
have
New Zealand taxpayer dollars
supporting New Zealand industry.
Where dual use is concerned, as well as strictly military
applications, of course, in technological terms, advances in
uncrewed technology can be helpful, for example, for agriculture
or other ways of
Kiwis
making a dollar and providing goods and services to their fellow Kiwis.
And that's not inconsistent with the surveillance or search that might
take place from the same unmanned aerial vehicles, for example.
So, that's one category. I think the social license is important.
At a broad level, of course, in terms of New Zealand's independent foreign policy,
that will be a subject that will be more helpfully explored with the Minister of
Foreign Affairs, whom I understand you'll be meeting with later.
But I think that's worthwhile just to place in context that any discussions with
the United States of America or any other sovereign nation,
of course,
are subject to that general understanding and agreement that we have as New
Zealanders, that we have an independent foreign policy. We are a sovereign nation.
We do make our own decisions as to the way that we deploy our defense
force, but of course, also the decisions we make ourselves with that.
In terms of the
reflections about appropriate levels of defense expenditure,
as I say, the New Zealand Government, on behalf of the New Zealand people, makes
its own decisions in that regard. We have come to the conclusion as a government,
and as I say, grateful for the fact that it appears to be broadly supported,
that 2% of our GDP expenditure is the right level for
us to attain by the year 2032/33, and that's why we're
getting on and doing exactly that.
I would actually just say, in relation to the comments reported,
made by Secretary of War Pete Hegseth at the same
dialogue, defense dialogue that I attended recently, that my response,
very shortly following the remarks that he made, was along the lines that we
have the best people, and I hear that consistently, and others have heard
that too, in terms of
our Navy, Army, and Air Force.
We are present everywhere. And yes, we know that our platforms do
need upgrading and indeed replacing in some cases.
But again, that's a decision we've already made in terms that we are
comfortable with.
Right.
I guess probably my concern there is if they continue
to push,
and we concede to them, then they'll ask for something else, and they'll ask for
something else. So, if you could give the committee comfort that
whenever these things arise, with the Americans in particular,
that is the position that you'll be sharing with them.
Yeah. I'm happy to restate
the comments that I have made since that Shangri-La Dialogue, and even as
recently as a couple of minutes ago.
Happy to state very clearly for the record that the position of this government
is that we will make decisions that we consider to be the
right ones for the New Zealand people and that our ideals and interests,
consistent with our independent foreign policy in terms of the deployment of any
such assets.
And that the comments or the views of other nations
are interesting so far as they go, but they're not determinative of the
positions and the decisions that we make.
Okay. I've got a quick supplementary from Damien O'Connor-
Yeah, please
... and then we'll jump into the more substantive discussion on DC.
Thank you. Well, it's really on the one of expenditure.
Have you set a guideline on procurement so
that New Zealand industry and New Zealand manufacturing
and other parts of our economy will have access to some opportunities?
And if so, what is the KPI that you're going to set?
Yeah. There's much that we can say and have been doing along those lines.
To avoid hearing my voice for two hours straight, perhaps at this point I might
look in the direction of Secretary of Defense for any comments he might want to
make.
Thank you, Minister. Yes, is the short answer.
For example,
prime suppliers, so you're kind of talking about Lockheed Martin or the
kind of big multinationals now have to develop a national capability
plan that says what economic return will come to New
Zealand from a purchase. It's assessed against other bids.
It has a 10% weighting minimum.
So, if you're taking a whole lot of factors, price and schedule and competence,
there's a significant weighting around what are they going to
deliver to New Zealand and New Zealand industry.
That's the first
point. The second is a whole lot of kind of related,
if you think about an ecosystem, so a
technology accelerator to try and kickstart New Zealand firms
into that higher tech area. The ability for New Zealand firms
themselves to be what are called thin primes, which is to act as a
prime, to act as the major contractor, and then
subcontract to Lockheed Martin, not the other way around.
That's another step we're taking to try and reinforce
New Zealand industry. So, there's a
range of-
Of interventions which are now in place to ensure,
if I come back to your opening question around bipartisanship and social
licence, New Zealanders have to know that a proportion of
that defence spend is going back into the pockets of
New Zealanders, and we're very alert to that and acting
accordingly.
Thank you.
Just to reiterate, as we build the case or the acquisition
program for a new capability that we need,
compatibility and interoperability with Australia is one of the first
questions we ask. But also right up there in those first questions is,
is this capability available in New Zealand already, or can we help New
Zealand companies develop and field this
capability for us? And we're
really behind that.
If I could just add, sorry, to that answer.
One of the things that we have tended to do in defence over decades is to say we
want to buy a ship,
so we look for a ship.
And sometimes it does have to be a ship or a
maritime helicopter.
But increasingly, we're wanting to go to New Zealand industry and
industry more generally to say, "This is the
effect we want.
We want to be able to deliver humanitarian assistance and
disaster relief across a coral atoll.
You tell us how that might best be done." That gives New Zealand
industry a better chance to play in a space that they are
better at, which is not building frigates, but coming up
with innovative ideas to deliver effect.
So we're trying to have open-ended questions at the start
so that New Zealand industry can say, "Well, hang on.
It doesn't just have to be the big players here.
There's a chance for us to come up with something novel,
and let's pitch it
to the government."
Very good. On that, I think that's a good opportunity to talk
more about the opportunity of uncrewed systems and drone-type technologies,
obviously, in
regards to your comments there,
SecDef, around
not specifying the
physical capability, but more the outcome.
What
aspect or what role do you see these systems playing and
New Zealand industry within there?
We've got some great new companies doing some incredible work
already domestically here, and obviously that's a part of the
DCP as well.
Yeah. It's a great opportunity to give effect
to that broad
structure of decision-making that the Secretary of Defence has
quite rightly outlined,
because it's now the case, particularly through uncrewed technologies,
that
we have the ability to answer questions about the kind of effect we want to provide
in a way that reflects the availability of
platforms that aren't necessarily large, so-called exquisite platforms
that can be all things to all people.
And so I think across the different domains,
in air,
on the surface of the sea, the subsurface, and of course,
land-based drones,
which is to say
some kind of uncrewed, unmanned, autonomous, or remotely controlled systems.
We have
those in New Zealand already producing that.
And in addition to saying that this technology exists in New Zealand,
is used in a dual-use fashion already for
other purposes.
Also, it's been employed elsewhere in the defense sense by New Zealand companies,
for example, in defense of Ukraine against
Russia's illegal invasion of that nation.
I think it's worth encouraging
the continued development
through the mechanism of the Defense Technology Accelerator that SecDef
has referred to. But of course, by engaging
closely with understanding
and ultimately where appropriate contracting with such
business entities. The Defense Capability Plan, as I sense we're sort
of moving towards that particular section of the
hearing,
does include provision for assistant surveillance, as
it's known,
and including
maritime, well, air surveillance, excuse me, of
regions
very far south of New Zealand to be polar capable in terms of that
capability. Often, of course, we think about New Zealand as
a series of islands on a map. Beyond that, we have, of course, our
territorial sea at 12 nautical miles, 200 nautical mile exclusive
economic zone, the massive search and rescue area out to the Pacific.
But we need to be mindful too, and continue to be present in the Antarctic
to protect, of course, New Zealand's scientific endeavors in that part of the
world. So if the committee's minded to hear more about
what kind of capability could be provided with those uncrewed technologies,
then Chief of Defense Force or his delegates would be more knowledgeable, of
course, than me. But I'll leave that to you, Mr.
Chair-
... as to whether you want to call him now.
Yeah, please.
I'd point immediately to the great success we've had
with our initial foray into uncrewed systems,
commercially known as the Bluebottle, but our Navy has
shared acquisition with other agencies in New Zealand of
two uncrewed systems that have been out and
about
New Zealand waters and the waters of our friends in the
Pacific and the waters in between.
Just to give you an example of that,
the two vessels we've named Tahia
and Rua,
have spent 221 days- At sea on behalf of
defence, customs, and police.
They have processed hundreds of contacts of vessels of interest
in the time that we have had them.
That includes up in Fiji,
with maritime domain awareness there of vessels that are moving,
either for good reason or suspiciously in and out of the EZs
of Fiji. Used
extensively, in particular up in Northland for the
seasonal yacht arrival
period,
and also
in the Bay of Plenty respectively.
So they

the, the, the numbers

extended? I mean,
given-
